$comment: Example 2: Moderate complexity — add a password reset flow to an auth service

intent.goal_statement: Add a secure password reset flow to the authentication service
intent.motivation: Users who forget their passwords currently have no self-service recovery path, generating support tickets
intent.success_definition: Users can request a password reset via email and complete it through a secure token-verified flow
intent.scope_context: Node.js/Fastify auth service with PostgreSQL and an existing email notification module

in_scope: Password reset token model and storage
out_of_scope: Password strength policy changes (existing policy applies)
Frontend SPA changes (separate team owns UI)
assumptions: Email notification module supports templated HTML emails
PostgreSQL has a `password_resets` table or can add one via migration
Existing JWT infrastructure can be reused for signed reset tokens
open_questions: What is the desired token expiry duration? (Proposal: 15 minutes)
Should reset-request reveal whether an email exists in the system? (Proposal: No — always return success to prevent enumeration)

approach.overview: Implement a two-phase reset flow: (1) request endpoint generates a signed token, stores its hash, and emails a reset link; (2) confirm endpoint verifies the token, checks expiry, and updates the password.
approach.rationale: The two-phase approach with hashed token storage is the OWASP-recommended pattern. It avoids storing raw tokens in the database and allows single-use enforcement.

alternatives_considered.approach: Use OTP (one-time password) via email instead of a link
alternatives_considered.rejection_reason: OTP requires users to manually transcribe a code; link-based flow has better conversion

alternatives_considered.approach: Reuse existing JWT infrastructure for reset tokens
alternatives_considered.rejection_reason: JWTs are stateless and harder to revoke/expire early; database-tracked tokens give us single-use and explicit invalidation

steps.title: Create password reset database migration
steps.description: Add a `password_resets` table with columns: id, user_id, token_hash, expires_at, used_at, created_at. Add index on token_hash.
steps.rationale: Token hash storage enables single-use and expiry enforcement
steps.acceptance_criteria: Migration runs successfully up and down; table exists with correct columns and indexes

steps.title: Implement token generation and verification utility
steps.description: Create a utility module that generates a cryptographically random token, returns the raw token + SHA-256 hash, and provides a verify function that hashes an input token and looks it up.
steps.rationale: Separating token logic from route handlers makes it testable
steps.acceptance_criteria: Unit tests pass: generate produces different tokens; verify rejects invalid/expired/used tokens

steps.title: Implement POST /auth/reset-request endpoint
steps.description: Accept email, look up user, generate token, store hash, send email with reset link. Always return 200 regardless of whether email exists. Apply rate limiting (5 requests per email per hour).
steps.rationale: Non-revealing responses prevent user enumeration attacks
steps.inputs: Token utility from s2, email notification module
steps.acceptance_criteria: Endpoint returns 200 for known and unknown emails; reset email is sent for known users; rate limit returns 429 after threshold

steps.title: Implement POST /auth/reset-confirm endpoint
steps.description: Accept token + new password, verify token, check expiry, validate password against existing policy, hash password, update user record, mark token as used.
steps.rationale: Single-use token enforcement prevents replay attacks
steps.inputs: Token utility from s2, existing password hashing module
steps.acceptance_criteria: Valid token + password resets the password; invalid/expired/used tokens are rejected; token is marked used after successful reset

steps.title: Write integration tests for full reset flow
steps.description: Test the complete flow: request reset → receive email → confirm reset → login with new password. Include edge cases: expired token, reused token, unknown email, rate limit.
steps.rationale: The reset flow touches auth, database, and email — integration coverage is essential
steps.inputs: Both endpoints from s3 and s4
steps.acceptance_criteria: All tests pass including edge cases; test coverage for the reset module is ≥ 90%

constraints.description: Reset tokens must be single-use and expire within 30 minutes
constraints.validation_method: Automated test attempting token reuse fails; automated test with expired token fails

constraints.description: Reset-request endpoint must not reveal whether an email is registered
constraints.validation_method: Response body and status code are identical for registered and unregistered emails

success_criteria.description: User can complete the full reset flow end-to-end in staging
Unknown emails receive the same response as known emails

risks.title: Email delivery delays could cause tokens to expire before user clicks
risks.description: If the email service has latency, users may receive reset links close to expiry
risks.mitigation: Set token TTL to 15 minutes (generous for normal delivery); add 'if your link has expired, request a new one' messaging in email template
risks.contingency: Increase TTL to 30 minutes if email latency is consistently high in production

risks.title: Rate limiter could block legitimate users behind shared NAT
risks.description: Corporate users sharing an IP could hit the rate limit collectively
risks.mitigation: Rate limit per email, not per IP
risks.contingency: Adjust rate limit threshold based on production metrics

dependencies.description: Email notification module must support HTML templates
PostgreSQL database accessible from the auth service

step_overrides.rationale: Endpoint design affects security posture — human review of response behavior and rate limiting

revision_history.change_summary: Initial plan: two-phase reset flow with hashed token storage
